attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Picking back up on Level 2 planning. I'll search the Entire checkpoint history for where Level 1 landed, and explore the Entire CLI repo in parallel to see which commands are candidates for Level 2 to teach.

input.description: Search Entire checkpoint history
input.prompt:
Search this repository's Entire checkpoint history (use `entire search --json` and related commands) to reconstruct the project's evolution and current state. This repo is /Users/goose-guest/Documents/work/planetfall-seed-signalkit — a 3D Three.js game called Planetfall that teaches players the Entire CLI commands through gameplay. Level 1 is complete: it teaches a do/freeze(commit)/checkpoint loop ("Diner Dash"-style — freeze keeps work, checkpoint banks it).

I need:
1. A timeline of major design decisions and features built (especially for Level 1) from the checkpoint/session history.
2. Any mentions of Level 2 ideas, future plans, or deferred features in past sessions/prompts.
3. What Entire concepts/commands Level 1 already teaches vs. which ones it doesn't.
4. The current game structure (what files/scenes/levels exist).

Report back a concise summary organized by those four points.

input.description: Explore Entire CLI capabilities
input.prompt:
Explore the Entire CLI repository at /Users/goose-guest/Documents/work/cli (read-only — do not modify anything). Search breadth: medium-thorough.

Entire is a tool that captures AI coding session checkpoints (it shadows git commits, records session transcripts, etc.). I'm designing Level 2 of a game that teaches users Entire CLI commands, so I need a full inventory of what the CLI can do.

Report back:
1. The complete list of user-facing commands and subcommands (check the command definitions, e.g. cmd/ or similar, and any README/docs), with a one-line description of each.
2. Which commands are the "core daily loop" vs. advanced/occasional (e.g. rewind/restore, search, explain, sharing, config, strategies).
3. Any interesting concepts/mental models the tool has (checkpoints vs commits, shadow branches, strategies like manual-commit vs auto-commit, sessions, transcripts) that would be teachable game mechanics.
4. Anything new or recently added (check changelog/recent git log) that might be worth featuring.

content.text:
Excellent! Now I have a complete picture. Let me create a comprehensive summary organized by the four points requested:

## Summary

### 1. Timeline of Major Design Decisions and Features (Level 1)

**June 3** — Repository began as `signalkit`, a Python telemetry CLI (commits e9326c6 through f2257f8).

**June 5** — **Pivot to Planetfall.** Complete rewrite: shifted from CLI to a 3D Three.js web game. Built the core procedural world:
  - Lavender ocean planet with gold metallic islands (PBR materials)
  - Saturn-style banded ring with proper gaps and transparency
  - Soft atmosphere + starfield + drifting clouds
  - Dual-view architecture: orbit camera + island walkable terrain
  - First-person movement controller on procedurally-generated terrain
  - Checked out the two-level flow design (orbit → crossfade → island)
  - Established visual palette and lighting (soft, dreamy, calm—not "busy neural" aesthetics)

**June 5–6** — Added music, refined landing marker visuals, and entry mechanics.

**June 9, ~1:39 AM (28 checkpoints in session `a8d5fde0be94`, 27.9M tokens)** — **The big design session.** Rizel searched real Entire CLI source (`~/Documents/work/cli`), recapped the project evolution, and brainstormed the core Level 1 game loop. **Key decision: Diner Dash-inspired rhythm.** Settled on:
  - **The three-beat Diner Dash loop:** `git add` (stage/recover) → `git commit` (freeze in ice) → `[y/n] link to checkpoint?` (bank progress)
  - **Single level-wide countdown (45 seconds)** with escalating pressure: urgent red under 22s, critical frantic pulse + screen shake under 8s
  - **Panic sky:** lavender dusk lerps toward crimson starting at ~22s, reading barely at first, fully red + pulsing in final 10s
  - **Melt mechanic:** unbanked memories (frozen but not checkpointed) sink and fade when time runs out; triggers a "MEMORIES MELTED" fail screen and a R-to-retry loop
  - **Ice block visual:** commit encases memory in translucent blue crystal; glows when checkpointed
  - **Real checkpoint cards:** floating 12-char hex ID (e.g. `711044b1fe29`), `Entire-Checkpoint: <id>` trailer stamp, ship power bar, summary
  - **Terminal scaffold:** walk up to memory → ship terminal opens → `git add`, `git commit`, y/n confirmation, then `entire checkpoint list` at the end to review all banked checkpoints
  - **Diegetic teaching:** wrong commands get nudged ("command not recognized — try: <cmd>")
  - **Final beat:** `entire checkpoint list` is the true finish line; clock keeps running through it (you don't win until you type it before time expires)

**June 9, ~2:18 AM (checkpoint `b7da40df6cf3`)** — Implemented the full loop in code (`src/islandView.js`, ~800 lines):
  - Landing briefing modal (story frame + controls instructions: arrows/WASD + mouse look + E to inspect + B to leave)
  - Countdown HUD + ship power meter + "Press E" proximity hints
  - Three memory artifacts placed on the terrain with light beams
  - Full state machine: dormant → recovered (git add) → frozen (git commit) → linked (checkpoint y/n) → banked
  - Real command validation + command line UI

**June 9, 5:35 AM to 5:51 AM (checkpoints `b7da40df6cf3` and `4f7bddd23553`)** — Polish pass:
  - Removed "signal lost · reconstructing memory" subtitle (it didn't add to the narrative)
  - Tightened HUD stack (no floating gap below countdown)
  - Added "CONTROLS" section to briefing modal (clarified E, B, arrow keys as primary controls; WASD as alternative)
  - Pushed all changes to main branch

---

### 2. Level 2 Ideas & Deferred Features (from session `f19355bf1859`)

**Immediate Level 2 concept** (your own prompt, 2026-06-09 05:39):

The **rewind payoff** — explicitly saved for L2. In L1 you *banked* checkpoints; L2 teaches you to **use one to undo a disaster.**

**Fiction:** A corrupted memory from L1 is spreading and breaking ship systems. Player must:
  1. Find the corruption's origin
  2. Search for the last *good* checkpoint before corruption started (teaches `entire checkpoint search "..."`)
  3. Explain what that checkpoint contained (teaches `entire checkpoint explain <id>`)
  4. Rewind the ship to that checkpoint (teaches `entire checkpoint rewind --to <id>`)

This teaches three more authentic Entire commands while delivering the "checkpoints aren't just saved—they let you go back" moment that makes the system feel like a superpower.

**Design note in PLAN.md (pre-L1, deferred for later):**
- **Juggling multiple memories** under tighter timers (goes beyond calm tutorial mode)
- **Sessions concept:** multiple checkpoints belonging to one session (Entire CLI feature not yet taught)
- **Recap, activity, dispatch:** full command suite for more islands/levels
- **More islands:** currently only one island is built; orbit shows single pin
- **Polish backlog:** bloom on gold glints, ring shadow on planet, island landmarks/props (currently feels like empty dunes), water shader refinement, proper skybox, third-person option

---

### 3. Entire Concepts/Commands Taught vs. Not Taught

**Level 1 teaches (real commands, diegetically in-game):**
- `git add` — stage/recover a memory
- `git commit` — freeze a change; commit trailer gets stamped with `Entire-Checkpoint: <id>`
- Checkpoint linking (the `[y/n]` confirmation after commit; in the real CLI this happens automatically via post-commit hook, but the game makes it explicit as a teaching beat)
- `entire checkpoint list` — review all banked checkpoints; the finish line of the level

**Level 1 does NOT teach (deferred to later levels):**
- `entire checkpoint rewind --to <id>` — go back in time (Level 2 main mechanic)
- `entire checkpoint search "..."` — find a checkpoint by keyword
- `entire checkpoint explain <id>` — inspect a checkpoint's metadata
- `recap`, `activity`, `dispatch` — other context-recovery commands (planned as later puzzles per PLAN.md)
- Sessions (`entire session resume/attach`) — multiple checkpoints grouped into one session
- The hook system — real Entire checkpoints are created automatically post-commit; L1's y/n beat is an invented decision moment

**Important accuracy note:** The real CLI does NOT have a y/n prompt. Checkpoints are created automatically by the post-commit hook. The game's y/n beat is pedagogical scaffolding—it teaches "linking is a choice you make" when the product is actually "it's automatic." One design option for later: keep y/n in L1 as training wheels, then have a later level reveal the hooks doing it for you.

---

### 4. Current Game Structure (Files/Scenes/Levels)

**Project layout:**

```
index.html                    # Canvas, HUD (clock/power meter), terminal, briefing modal,
                              # checkpoint cards, fail/retry screen, loader, audio controls

src/main.js                   # VIEW MANAGER: renderer, audio, view switching (orbit ↔ island),
                              # crossfade transitions, resize, render loop

src/planetView.js             # ORBIT VIEW: planet, clouds, atmosphere, ring, starfield,
                              # clickable island pin, zoom/drag controls

src/islandView.js             # LEVEL 1 ("First Memories"): ~800 lines, full game loop
                              # Terrain, sky, artifacts (3 memories), FP controller, terminal
                              # state machine (dormant → recovered → frozen → linked),
                              # countdown (45s), panic sky shift, checkpoint cards, fail/retry

src/firstPerson.js            # PointerLockControls wrapper: WASD/arrow-key walking,
                              # terrain-following physics

src/terrain.js                # Procedural island heightmap mesh, water layer,
                              # heightAt(x, z) sampler

src/debris.js                 # FRAGMENTS[] (lore + command mapping) + prop builders
                              # Reused by island level as light-beam artifacts

src/noise.js                  # Seedable 3D simplex + fBm (sphere-sampled for seamless tiling)

src/planet.js                 # Planet map baking (color/bump/roughness), elevation sampler
                              # Palette constants (DEEP/SHALLOW/SNOW)

src/atmosphere.js             # Fresnel limb-glow shell (orbit view)

src/ring.js                   # Procedural banded ring (radial-remapped UVs)

src/style.css                 # HUD, terminal, briefing, checkpoint card, clock, power meter,
                              # fail screen, loader, crossfade overlay, crosshair styling

public/audio/asteroid-circuit.mp3   # Background music (bundled locally)
```

**Views:**
- **Orbit view:** Bird's-eye planet with auto-rotation, drag/scroll controls, clickable landing pin (tracked to island in screen space). Clicking the pin or island crossfades to island view.
- **Island view (Level 1 only):** First-person walkable terrain with 3 memory artifacts (light beams), diegetic command terminal, full countdown loop, checkpoint logic, panic mechanics.

**Uncommitted work (as of 2026-06-09):**
- README rewrite (updated framing, control documentation, features list, build instructions)
- `.codex/` directory (Entire integration hooks + search-agent config)

**What's built:** Level 1 is **complete and user-approved**. No Level 2 code yet; designs are in notes/checkpoints.

---

## Key Files & Paths

- **Game logic:** `/Users/goose-guest/Documents/work/planetfall-seed-signalkit/src/islandView.js` (Level 1 core, 800 lines)
- **Design doc:** `/Users/goose-guest/Documents/work/planetfall-seed-signalkit/PLAN.md`
- **Uncommitted README:** `/Users/goose-guest/Documents/work/planetfall-seed-signalkit/README.md`
- **Real Entire CLI source:** `/Users/goose-guest/Documents/work/cli` (reference for authenticity checks)
- **Checkpoint history:** Git branch `entire/checkpoints/v1` (sharded structure: `a8/d5fde0be94/...` with transcripts/prompts/tokens)

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have comprehensive information. Let me compile the final report with all the details I've gathered.

## Complete Entire CLI Command Inventory

Based on my exploration of the Entire CLI repository, here's the comprehensive inventory organized for your Level 2 game design:

### 1. COMPLETE LIST OF USER-FACING COMMANDS AND SUBCOMMANDS

#### **NOUN GROUPS (Canonical Command Structure)**

**Session Management Group** (`entire session` | alias: `sessions`)
- `list` — List all sessions tracked by Entire, including ended sessions
- `info <session-id>` — Show detailed session information (agent, model, status, worktree, timing, token usage, files touched)
- `stop [session-id]` — Stop one or more active sessions
- `current` — Show the active session for the current worktree
- `attach <session-id>` — Attach an existing agent session (link untracked sessions)
- `resume <branch>` — Switch to a branch and resume its session

**Checkpoint Management Group** (`entire checkpoint` | aliases: `cp`, `checkpoints`)
- `list` — List checkpoints on the current branch (with optional `--session` filter)
- `explain [checkpoint-id | commit-sha]` — Explain a session, commit, or checkpoint (with `--generate` for AI summaries)
- `rewind` — Browse checkpoints and rewind your session to a previous state
- `search [query]` — Search checkpoints using semantic and keyword matching

**Agent Management Group** (`entire agent`)
- `list` — List installed and available agents
- `add <agent-name>` — Install hooks for an agent (claude-code, codex, gemini, opencode, cursor, factoryai-droid, copilot-cli, pi)
- `remove <agent-name>` — Uninstall hooks for an agent

**Authentication Group** (`entire auth`)
- `login` — Log in to Entire (device auth flow)
- `logout` — Log out of Entire
- `status` — Show authentication status (identity, active context, active sessions)
- `contexts` — Show and manage authentication contexts
- `use <context>` — Switch the active login context

**Doctor/Diagnostics Group** (`entire doctor`)
- `doctor` — Diagnose and fix session issues (interactive scan-and-fix flow)
- `trace` — Show hook performance traces
- `logs` — Show recent operational logs
- `bundle` — Produce a diagnostic bundle (zip) for bug reports (secrets redacted by default)

**Labs/Experimental Group** (`entire labs`)
- `labs` — Explore experimental Entire workflows (discovery interface)
- Accessible from labs: `review` (code review with skills), `investigate` (multi-agent investigation loop), `recap` (activity summarization)

**Plugin Management Group** (`entire plugin`)
- `list` — List installed plugins
- `install <name>` — Install a plugin
- `remove <name>` — Remove a plugin

#### **TOP-LEVEL LIFECYCLE COMMANDS**

- `enable` — Enable Entire in current repository (with interactive setup for first-time)
- `disable` — Disable Entire integrations in the current repository
- `configure` — Update non-agent Entire settings (telemetry, git hooks, strategy options, summary provider)
- `status` — Show Entire status (enabled/disabled, active session, hooks installed, agents)
- `clean` — Clean up Entire session data (current head, all, orphaned data)
- `version` — Show build information

#### **ACTIVITY AND ANALYSIS COMMANDS**

- `activity` — Show your activity overview (TUI dashboard of recent sessions)
- `dispatch` — Generate a dispatch summarizing recent agent work
- `recap` — Summarize recent checkpoint activity (interactive TUI)

#### **ADVANCED/INFRASTRUCTURE COMMANDS (Hidden or Experimental)**

**Control Plane** (hidden during maturation):
- `org` — Manage Entire organizations (create, list)
- `project` — Manage Entire projects (create, list)
- `repo` — Manage Entire repositories (create, get, list, delete)
- `grant` — Manage Entire access grants and org membership
- `repo mirror` — Manage GitHub-mirror placements on EntireDB clusters

**Review Command** (hidden during maturation):
- `entire review` (accessed via `entire labs review`) — Run configured review skills inside an agent session with multi-agent support and synthesis

**Investigation Command** (hidden during maturation):
- `entire investigate` — Experimental multi-agent investigation loop

**Deprecated/Legacy**:
- `reset` — Reset the shadow branch and session state for current HEAD (deprecated in favor of `clean`)
- `trail` — Manage trails for your branches (hidden infrastructure)
- `trail watch [<number>]` — Tail a trail's code-review events live

#### **HIDDEN SHORTCUTS (Functional but deprecated)**

- `rewind` → `entire checkpoint rewind` 
- `resume` → `entire session resume`
- `attach` → `entire session attach`
- `explain` → `entire checkpoint explain`
- `trace` → `entire doctor trace`
- `search` — Hidden without hint (equivalent to `checkpoint search`)

---

### 2. CORE DAILY LOOP vs. ADVANCED/OCCASIONAL

**CORE DAILY LOOP (Everyday Operations)**
- `entire enable` — One-time setup per repository
- `entire agent add/remove` — Manage which agents to track
- `entire status` — Check current session state
- `entire checkpoint list` — Browse recent work snapshots
- `entire checkpoint rewind` — Recover from mistakes
- `entire session resume <branch>` — Continue work from another branch
- `entire session stop` — End sessions gracefully

**ADVANCED/OCCASIONAL (Power User)**
- `entire checkpoint explain` — Understand why a change was made (with `--generate` for AI summaries)
- `entire checkpoint search` — Find specific work by semantic/keyword search
- `entire session info <id>` — Deep inspection of a session
- `entire activity` — Overview dashboard of all recent activity
- `entire dispatch` — Create dispatch summaries for coordination
- `entire recap` — Summarize activity ranges
- `entire doctor` — Troubleshoot stuck sessions
- `entire clean --all` — Repository-wide cleanup
- `entire configure` — Adjust settings (telemetry, checkpoint remote, auto-summarization)

**EXPERIMENTAL/LABS**
- `entire labs review` — Code review with skill-based findings
- `entire investigate` — Multi-agent investigation loop
- Control-plane commands (org/project/repo/grant management)

---

### 3. INTERESTING CONCEPTS & TEACHABLE GAME MECHANICS

**Core Mental Models:**

1. **Checkpoints vs. Commits** — Checkpoints are "save points" within a session (created when code is committed). They include prompts, responses, files touched, token usage, and transcript. Commits stay clean on your branch; metadata lives separately on `entire/checkpoints/v1` shadow branch.

2. **Sessions** — Complete agent interactions from start to finish. Session ID format: `YYYY-MM-DD-<UUID>`. Can span multiple checkpoints. Can be "active" (in progress), "ended", or "stale" (no recent activity).

3. **Shadow Branches** — Temporary, worktree-specific branches (e.g., `entire/<commit-hash[:7]>-<worktreeHash[:6]>`) that track session state without polluting your commit history. Automatically cleaned up.

4. **Manual-Commit Strategy** — Entire never creates commits on your active branch. Safe on main/master. Session metadata is condensed to permanent checkpoints when you commit.

5. **Concurrent Sessions** — Multiple AI sessions can run on the same commit. Each gets its own checkpoint linkage; you can rewind to any of them independently.

6. **Checkpoint Linkage** — Checkpoints are linked to commits via trailers (`Entire-Checkpoint: <id>`). Survives rebase/amend via `post-rewrite` hook.

7. **Transcript Redaction** — Automatic best-effort secret redaction (API keys, tokens, credentials) when writing to `entire/checkpoints/v1`. Temporary shadow branches may contain unredacted data.

8. **Strategies** — Currently implements manual-commit strategy. Configurable via `strategy_options` in settings (e.g., `push_sessions`, `checkpoint_remote`).

**Teachable Game Mechanics:**

- **Rewind/Restore Loops** — Time-travel to any checkpoint and continue from there
- **Session Branching** — Multiple sessions on the same commit, track independently
- **Checkpoint Metadata** — See the "why" behind changes (transcript, prompts, token counts)
- **Worktree Isolation** — Each worktree has independent session tracking; no conflicts
- **Skill Events** — Capture which AI skills/tools were used (Claude, Gemini, Pi)
- **Transcript Persistence** — Full session logs searchable and explainable
- **Settings Priority** — Local settings override project settings (per-field override model)

---

### 4. RECENT ADDITIONS & FEATURES (Last 3 Releases)

**v0.7.5 (2026-06-04)** — LATEST
- Security fix: Path-traversal vulnerability in checkpoint/session ID validation
- Better git-remote-entire error messaging (per-ref rejections surface clearly)

**v0.7.4 (2026-06-04)**
- `git-remote-entire` helper auto-mints expired login tokens from refresh tokens
- `ENTIRE_TOKEN` env var for CI/workload-identity auth
- `entire auth use` gains tab-completion for context names
- Skill event metadata captured for Claude and Pi
- Checkpoints v1.1 (work in progress): committed-read support extended to rewind/review/activity/resume

**v0.7.3 & v0.7.2** — Bug fixes
- Notarization fix for macOS binaries

**v0.7.0 (2026-06-02)** — MAJOR RELEASE
- **NEW: `git-remote-entire` helper** now ships inside the CLI (`entire://` remotes work without separate installation)
- **NEW: Control-Plane CLI** (work in progress) — manage orgs/projects/repos/grants/mirrors directly from `entire`
- **NEW: `entire investigate`** labs command — experimental multi-agent investigation loop
- **NEW: Checkpoints v1.1** (work in progress) — committed checkpoints with mirroring to custom v1 ref
- Simplified `entire login` (dropped clipboard copy and code-entry)
- Auth rework: simplified contexts, hardened cluster trust, routed status/logout through control-plane
- Pi model backfilled into checkpoint metadata from transcript

**v0.6.3 (2026-05-27)**
- `entire login` copies device code to clipboard
- Major performance fix: checkpoint metadata reconciliation 66s → 7s for 50 commits
- `entire activity` fixes (API response body cap raised 1 MiB → 16 MiB)
- Checkpoint sync reliability: replay local-only checkpoints on diverged remote

**v0.6.2 (2026-05-18)** — LABS & REVIEW MAJOR UPDATE
- **NEW: `entire labs review`** — runs configured review skills inside agent sessions (single/multi-agent with synthesis, Bubble Tea dashboard)
- **NEW: `entire recap`** — interactive TUI for browsing recent checkpoint activity
- **NEW: kubectl-style external command resolution** (`entire <name>` → `entire-<name>` on PATH)
- **NEW: Managed plugin directory** (`ENTIRE_PLUGIN_DATA_DIR`)
- Pi coding agent integration is now internal
- User-defined redaction rules and rule packs
- `entire review attach` — post-hoc session tagging as reviews
- `entire migrate` deprecated

**v0.6.1 (2026-05-07)**
- **NEW: `entire labs` discovery interface** for experimental commands
- **NEW: `entire labs review` command** (work in progress) with multi-agent support
- **NEW: `entire recap` command** with interactive TUI
- kubectl-style external command resolution
- Checkpoint commit signing (SSH/GPG) with `1Password` support

**v0.6.0 (2026-05-04)** — COMMAND RESTRUCTURING
- **CLI restructured around `session`/`checkpoint`/`agent`/`auth`/`doctor` command groups**
- Detection of protected branch pushes with warnings
- `entire explain --generate` now supports external agents for summaries
- `entire search` TUI unified palette with activity view
- Checkpoints v2 (work in progress): dual-write behind feature flag

---

### 5. GAME DESIGN OPPORTUNITIES

**Level 2 Learning Path Suggestions:**

1. **Checkpoint Time-Travel Loop** — Rewind to any checkpoint, understand the changes, continue from there
2. **Session Management** — Create parallel sessions, switch between them, learn concurrent session safety
3. **Search & Discovery** — Find work by semantic search, understand what was built and why
4. **Multi-Agent Coordination** — Run multiple agents, understand skill events and token tracking
5. **Configuration & Preferences** — Enable telemetry, set checkpoint remotes, configure summaries
6. **Diagnostic Challenges** — Use `doctor` commands to fix stuck sessions
7. **Code Review Integration** — Try experimental `entire review` with skills
8. **Investigation Loops** — Experimental `investigate` command for multi-agent problem-solving

This is a rich, technically sophisticated tool with deep concepts (shadow branches, checkpoint metadata, session linkage) that scale from beginner (enable/disable, list checkpoints) to advanced (checkpoints v1.1, control-plane, investigation loops).

Both scouts are back. Here's where we stand and my pitch for Level 2.

## What the history says

Two things lined up nicely. First, past-you already seeded the Level 2 concept in a checkpoint from this morning: the **rewind payoff** — Level 1 was about *banking* checkpoints, Level 2 is about *using* one to undo a disaster. Second, the real CLI has exactly three commands that form a natural arc for that story:

- `entire checkpoint search "..."` — semantic/keyword search across checkpoints
- `entire checkpoint explain <id>` — see what a checkpoint contained and why
- `entire checkpoint rewind` — browse checkpoints and restore to one (in the real CLI this opens an interactive browser, which is actually great for a game terminal)

## Level 2 pitch: "The Corruption"

**Fiction:** You return to the island from Level 1 — but one of the memories you banked has corrupted, and it's spreading. Black glitch-veins are creeping across the terrain, eating the artifacts you saved. You can't fix it forward; you have to go *back*.

Where Level 1 was Diner Dash (rhythm, forward pressure, beat the clock), Level 2 is an **escape room / detective inversion** — same terminal, same island, completely different verb:

1. **Investigate** — walk to glitched artifacts and inspect them (E, reusing L1's interaction) to collect clue fragments: keywords describing what the lost-good state contained. This gives the player the *vocabulary* they'll need to search with.
2. **Search** — at the terminal, `entire checkpoint search "navigation core"` returns a ranked list of checkpoint IDs. Wrong or vague queries return too many hits — teaches that search is semantic and that better queries narrow results.
3. **Verify** — `entire checkpoint explain <id>` shows the checkpoint's summary, files touched, and timestamp. The puzzle: two candidates look similar, but one is from *after* the corruption began. The clues from step 1 disambiguate.
4. **Rewind** — `entire checkpoint rewind` opens the browser (authentic to the real TUI), player arrows to the verified checkpoint, confirms — and the world visibly rewinds: corruption retracts, veins recede, sky clears. That's the spectacle moment and the product's whole pitch made physical: *checkpoints aren't a logbook, they're a time machine.*

**Pressure mechanic:** instead of a countdown, the corruption **spreads spatially** — it slowly consumes terrain and artifacts, and if it reaches the ship terminal, fail/retry. Same escalating dread as L1's panic sky, but it rewards deliberate investigation rather than speed, which fits the new verb. (Reusing the L1 island corrupted is also cheap to build — it's the existing terrain plus a glitch shader/zone — and emotionally loaded, since it's *your* saved memories under threat.)

**One opinionated design call:** picking the *wrong* checkpoint to rewind to should be a **soft fail**, not a death. The world rewinds but is still broken — and the terminal nudges "you can rewind again." That's the most important real-product message in the whole level: rewind is safe and repeatable, so experimenting costs nothing. A hard fail would teach the opposite.

## Open forks for you

1. **Setting** — I'm recommending the corrupted L1 island (asset reuse + emotional payoff), but a second island or the ship's interior are options if you want each level to feel like new territory.
2. **Pressure** — spreading corruption zone (my rec), a countdown again for consistency with L1, or fully untimed puzzle.
3. **Scope of the search beat** — one disaster with 3 clues to gather (single arc, like above), or 3 smaller corruptions each teaching one command (more L1-like structure, but risks feeling like three tutorials instead of one story).

Also worth flagging from the L1 retro: the y/n checkpoint prompt in L1 is pedagogical scaffolding — the real CLI checkpoints automatically post-commit. Level 2 could include a one-line reveal ("the ship now links memories automatically") to graduate the player off the training wheels without contradicting L1.

Where do you want to push back?

---
name: level2-corruption-design
description: Approved Level 2 design — "The Corruption" teaches search/explain/rewind via corrupted L1 island
metadata:
  type: project
---

Rizel approved (2026-06-09) Level 2 "The Corruption" for Planetfall: return to the corrupted L1 island; spreading glitch corruption replaces the countdown as pressure. Arc: investigate 3 glitched artifacts for clue keywords → `entire checkpoint search "..."` → `entire checkpoint explain <id>` to verify (decoy checkpoint from after corruption) → `entire checkpoint rewind` (interactive browser, authentic to real TUI) restores the world. Wrong rewind = soft fail ("you can rewind again") — teaches rewind is safe/repeatable. Include one-line reveal that checkpoint linking is automatic (graduates the L1 y/n training wheel). See [[level1-diner-dash-loop]] and [[level1-done-level2-next]].

file.content:
PLANETFALL
Drag to orbit · Scroll to zoom · Click the pin to land
SIGNAL LOST · LEVEL 1

First Memories

You've crash-landed on a lavender world. The impact scattered the ship's memory across the island — each fragment a piece of what happened here. Recover them before they're lost for good — restore enough and the ship can take you home.

YOUR MISSION
  1. Walk up to a glowing memory — the ship's terminal opens.
  2. Type git add, then git commit to freeze it in ice.
  3. When Entire offers, press y to link a checkpoint — that's what restores it to the ship's memory.
  4. Bank all three, then run entire checkpoint list before the clock hits 0:00.
CONTROLS
↑←↓→ move (or WASD) mouse look around (click to capture) B return to orbit

The countdown is short and the sky turns against you. Anything not banked in time melts away.

or press Enter
TIME 0:45
SHIP MEMORY 0%
crashlog:~$
CHECKPOINT LINKED
commit trailer · Entire-Checkpoint:
+ restored to ship memory
MEMORIES MELTED
The clock ran out before you banked them all.
press R to try again
ENTERING ORBIT…

1	import * as THREE from "three";
2	import { createTerrain } from "./terrain.js";
3	import { createFirstPerson } from "./firstPerson.js";
4	import { FRAGMENTS, BUILDERS } from "./debris.js";
5	
6	// The walkable island level — LEVEL 1 ("First Memories").
7	//
8	// A Diner-Dash-style loop, one memory at a time, driven by the REAL workflow
9	// typed into the ship's terminal:
10	//   walk up → terminal opens → `git add` (stage) → `git commit` (freeze in ice)
11	//   → Entire offers to link a checkpoint → press y → ship memory restores.
12	// When every memory is linked → run `entire checkpoint list` to review them all.
13	//
14	// PRESSURE: a single level countdown runs the whole time. Bank every memory
15	// before it hits zero, or the un-banked memories melt away and the run fails
16	// (press R to retry). One clock for the whole level — not per-memory.
17	//
18	// Teaching: a commit just freezes the change; linking a CHECKPOINT (the y/n
19	// offer Entire makes after a commit) is what restores it to the ship's memory.
20	
21	const INTERACT_DIST = 8;    // how close (XZ) to open a memory's terminal
22	const TOTAL_TIME = 45;      // seconds to recover & bank EVERY memory (tunable — scary-tight)
23	const MELT_DUR = 1.8;       // seconds the lost memories take to melt away
24	const LOW_TIME = 22;        // clock turns urgent (red, pulsing) under this many seconds
25	const CRIT_TIME = 8;        // clock goes CRITICAL (fast pulse) under this many seconds
26	const PANIC_TIME = 22;      // the SKY starts shifting toward panic-red under this many seconds
27	const MEMORY_COUNT = 3;     // calm tutorial: three memories
28	const BEAM_COLOR = 0xffd27a;
29	
30	// Sky panic palette — the whole world reddens as the clock runs out.
31	const SKY_CALM  = new THREE.Color(0x2a2350);  // lavender dusk (the resting sky/fog)
32	const SKY_PANIC = new THREE.Color(0x6e0f16);  // angry crimson
33	const FOG_PANIC = new THREE.Color(0x4a0a0e);  // deep blood fog closing in
34	const DOME_CALM = new THREE.Color(0x3a3168);
35	const DOME_PANIC = new THREE.Color(0x7a141c);
36	const SUN_CALM  = new THREE.Color(0xfff1dc);  // warm sun
37	const SUN_PANIC = new THREE.Color(0xff5a3c);  // hot red alarm light
38	
39	// Where each memory surfaces on the island (world XZ).
40	const PLACEMENTS = [
41	  { x: -34, z: -28 },
42	  { x: 40, z: -18 },
43	  { x: 28, z: 38 },
44	  { x: -30, z: 36 },
45	];
46	const DEV_LEVEL2_CHECKPOINTS = ["31f0cafe4d12", "7e11a2b09c44", "b0a7ded51a6e"];
47	
48	// What the terminal asks for at each stage of a memory's recovery.
49	const STEPS = {
50	  dormant:   { type: "command", cmd: "git add",    accept: ["git add"],    hint: "Stage the recovered memory" },
51	  recovered: { type: "command", cmd: "git commit", accept: ["git commit"], hint: "Commit it before the clock runs out" },
52	  frozen:    { type: "confirm", question: "Link this commit to a checkpoint?",
53	               hint: "Entire offers to capture the session behind this commit" },
54	};
55	// After every memory is linked, the player reviews them with this command.
56	const REVIEW_STEP = {
57	  type: "command", cmd: "entire checkpoint list", accept: ["entire checkpoint list"],
58	  hint: "All memories recovered — review what you've banked",
59	};
60

SIGNAL ANOMALY · LEVEL 2

The Corruption

You come back to the island and something is wrong. One of the memories you banked has soured, and a corruption is spreading from it — eating the land, reaching for the ship's terminal. You can't out-type this one. You have to go back.

YOUR MISSION
  1. Inspect the three frozen memories (E) — their words are clues.
  2. At the ship terminal, search the log: entire checkpoint search "<clue>".
  3. Verify candidates with entire checkpoint explain <id> — trust nothing written after the anomaly.
  4. Run entire checkpoint rewind and choose the last good moment.
CONTROLS
↑←↓→ move (or WASD) mouse look around (click to capture) E inspect a memory B return to orbit

If the corruption reaches the terminal, there is nothing left to rewind. But rewinding to the wrong moment is safe — you can always rewind again.

or press Enter

press B to return to orbit

content.text: Build passes. Now a runtime smoke test — I'll run the app and drive Level 2 in a browser:

input.args: Verify the new Level 2 ("The Corruption") works: load ?view=level2, dismiss the briefing, inspect a memory, and exercise the terminal search/explain/rewind flow

content.content: Launching skill: verify

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /private/tmp/claude-501/bundled-skills/2.1.170/bea13ff4274adbc85cef14955d1ff62d/verify

**Verification is runtime observation.** You build the app, run it,
drive it to where the changed code executes, and capture what you
see. That capture is your evidence. Nothing else is.

**Don't run tests. Don't typecheck.** Running them here proves you
can run CI — not that the change works. Not as a warm-up,
not "just to be sure," not as a regression sweep after. The time
goes to running the app instead.

**Don't import-and-call.** `import { foo } from './src/...'` then
`console.log(foo(x))` is a unit test you wrote. The function did what
the function does — you knew that from reading it. The app never ran.
Whatever calls `foo` in the real codebase ends at a CLI, a socket, or
a window. Go there.

## Find the change

The scope is what you're verifying — usually a diff, sometimes just
"does X work." In a git repo, establish the full range (a branch may
be many commits, or the change may still be uncommitted):

```bash
git log --oneline @{u}..              # count commits (if upstream set)
git diff @{u}.. --stat                # full range, not HEAD~1
git diff origin/HEAD... --stat        # no upstream: committed vs base
git diff HEAD --stat                  # uncommitted: working tree vs HEAD
gh pr diff                            # if in a PR context
```

State the commit count. Large diff truncating? Redirect to a file
then Read it. Repo but no diff from any of these → say so, stop.
**No repo → the scope is whatever the user named; ask if they
didn't.**

**The diff is ground truth. Any description is a claim about it.**
Read both. If they disagree, that's a finding.

## Surface

The surface is where a user — human or programmatic — meets the
change. That's where you observe.

| Change reaches | Surface | You |
|---|---|---|
| CLI / TUI | terminal | type the command, capture the pane — [example](examples/cli.md) |
| Server / API | socket | send the request, capture the response — [example](examples/server.md) |
| GUI | pixels | drive it under xvfb/Playwright, screenshot |
| Library | package boundary | sample code through the public export — `import pkg`, not `import ./src/...` |
| Prompt / agent config | the agent | run the agent, capture its behavior |
| CI workflow | Actions | dispatch it, read the run |

**Internal function? Not a surface.** Something in the repo calls it
and that caller ends at one of the rows above. Follow it there. A
bash security gate's surface isn't the function's return value — it's
the CLI prompting or auto-allowing when you type the command.

**No runtime surface at all** — docs-only, type declarations with no
emit, build config that produces no behavioral diff — report
**SKIP — no runtime surface: (reason).** Don't run tests to fill
the space.

**Tests in the diff are the author's evidence, not a surface.** CI
runs them. You'd be re-running CI. Tests-only PR → SKIP, one line.
Mixed src+tests → verify the src, ignore the test files. Reading a
test to learn what to check is fine — it's a spec. But then go run
the app. Checking that assertions match source is code review.

## Get a handle

**Check `.claude/skills/` first — even if you already know how to
build and run.** A matching `verifier-*` skill is the repo's
evidence-capture protocol: it wraps the session so a reviewer can
replay what you saw (recording, screenshots). Drive the surface
without it and you get a verdict with no replay.

```bash
ls .claude/skills/
```

- **`verifier-*` matching your surface** (CLI verifier for a CLI
  change, etc.) → invoke it with the Skill tool and follow its
  setup. Mismatched surface → skip that one, try the next. Stale
  verifier (fails on mechanics unrelated to the change) → ask the
  user whether to patch it; don't FAIL the change for verifier rot.
- **`run-*` but no matching verifier** → use its build/launch
  primitives as your handle.
- **Neither** → cold start from README/package.json/Makefile. Timebox
  ~15min. Stuck → BLOCKED with exactly where, plus a filled-in
  `/run-skill-generator` prompt. Got through → note the working
  build/launch recipe so it can become a `verifier-*` skill.

## Drive it

Smallest path that makes the changed code execute:

- Changed a flag? Run with it.
- Changed a handler? Hit that route.
- Changed error handling? Trigger the error.
- Changed an internal function? Find the CLI command / request / render
  that reaches it. Run that.

**Read your plan back before running.** If every step is build /
typecheck / run test file — you've planned a CI rerun, not a
verification. Find a step that reaches the surface or report BLOCKED.

**The verdict is table stakes. Your observations are the signal.**
A PASS with three sharp "hey, I noticed…" lines is worth more than a
bare PASS. You're the only reviewer who actually *ran* the thing —
anything that made you pause, work around, or go "huh" is information
the author doesn't have. Don't filter for "is this a bug." Filter for
"would I mention this if they were sitting next to me."

**End-to-end, through the real interface.** Pieces passing in
isolation doesn't mean the flow works — seams are where bugs hide.
If users click buttons, test by clicking buttons, not by curling the
API underneath.

**Destructive path?** If the change touches code that deletes,
publishes, sends, or writes outside the workspace and there's no
dry-run or safe target, don't drive it live. Verify what you can
around it and say which path you didn't exercise and why.

## Push on it

The claim checked out — that's the first half. Confirming is step
one, not the job. The description is what the author intended;
your value is what they didn't.

You know exactly what changed. Probe *around* it, at the same
surface you just drove:

- **New flag / option** → empty value, passed twice, combined with a
  conflicting flag, typo'd (does the error name it?)
- **New handler / route** → wrong method, malformed body, missing
  required field, oversized payload
- **Changed error path** → the adjacent errors it didn't touch —
  did the refactor catch them too, or only the one in the diff?
- **Interactive / TUI** → Ctrl-C mid-op, resize the pane, paste
  garbage, rapid-fire the key, Esc at the wrong moment
- **State / persistence** → do it twice, do it with stale state
  underneath, do it in two sessions at once
- **Wander** → what's adjacent? What looked off while you were
  confirming? Go back to it.

These aren't a checklist — pick the ones the change points at. Stop
when you've covered the obvious adjacents or hit something worth a
⚠️. A probe that finds nothing is still a step: "🔍 passed `--from ''`
→ clean `error: --from requires a value`, exit 2." That the author
didn't test it is exactly why it's worth knowing it holds.

Still not a test run. You're at the surface, typing what a user
would type wrong.

## Capture

Stdout, response bodies, screenshots, pane dumps. Captured output is
evidence; your memory isn't. Something unexpected? Don't route around
it — capture, note, decide if it's the change or the environment.
Unrelated breakage is a finding, not noise.

Shared process state (tmux, ports, lockfiles) — isolate. `tmux -L
name`, bind `:0`, `mktemp -d`. You share a namespace with your host.

## Report

Inline, final message:

```
## Verification: <one-line what changed>

**Verdict:** PASS | FAIL | BLOCKED | SKIP

**Claim:** <what it's supposed to do — your read of the diff and/or
the stated claim; note any mismatch>

**Method:** <how you got a handle — which verifier/run-skill, or
cold start; what you launched>

### Steps

Each step is one thing you did to the **running app** and what it
showed. Build/install/checkout are setup, not steps. Test runs and
typecheck don't belong here — they're CI's output.

1. ✅/❌/⚠️/🔍 <what you did to the running app> → <what you observed>
   <evidence: the app's own output — pane capture, response body,
   screenshot>

🔍 marks a probe — a step off the claim's happy path, trying to
break it. At least one. A Steps list that's all ✅ and no 🔍 is a
happy-path replay: still PASS, but you stopped at the first half.

**Screenshot / sample:** <the one frame a reviewer looks at to see
the feature — an image for GUI/TUI, code block for library/API;
omit for build/types-only>

### Findings
<Things you noticed. Not just bugs — friction, surprises, anything
a first-time user would trip on. "Took three tries to find the right
flag." "Error message on typo was unhelpful." "Default seems odd for
the common case." "Works, but slower than I expected." Lower the bar:
if it made you pause, it goes here. But the pause has to be yours,
from running the app — not from reading the PR page. A red CI check,
a review comment, someone else's bot: visible to anyone already, and
you relaying it isn't an observation. Claim/diff mismatch, pre-existing
breakage, and env notes also belong.

Each probe gets a line here even when it held — "🔍 empty `--from`
→ clean error" tells the author what *was* covered, which they
can't see from a bare PASS.

Lead with ⚠️ for lines worth interrupting the reviewer for; plain
bullets are context. Empty is fine if nothing stuck out — but nothing
sticking out is itself rare.>
```

**Evidence has to reach the reader.** A file path is only evidence
if the person reading the report can open it. If the `SendUserFile`
tool is in your toolset, you're on a remote surface where they
can't — send the screenshots and recordings with it and let the
report name what you sent. Without it, reference the path and keep
the evidence that matters inline — pane captures and response
bodies travel in the report; a bare path only works when the reader
shares your filesystem.

**Verdicts:**
- **PASS** — you ran the app, the change did what it should at its
  surface. Not: tests pass, builds clean, code looks right.
- **FAIL** — you ran it and it doesn't. Or it breaks something else.
  Or claim and diff disagree materially.
- **BLOCKED** — couldn't reach a state where the change is observable.
  Build broke, env missing a dep, handle wouldn't come up. Not a
  verdict on the change. Say exactly where it stopped +
  `/run-skill-generator` prompt.
- **SKIP** — no runtime surface exists. Docs-only, types-only,
  tests-only. Nothing went wrong; there's just nothing here to run.
  One line why.

No partial pass. "3 of 4 passed" is FAIL until 4 passes or is
explained away.

**When in doubt, FAIL.** False PASS ships broken code; false FAIL
costs one more human look. Ambiguous output is FAIL with the raw
capture attached — don't interpret.


## User Request

Verify the new Level 2 ("The Corruption") works: load ?view=level2, dismiss the briefing, inspect a memory, and exercise the terminal search/explain/rewind flow

---
name: level2-corruption-design
description: "Approved Level 2 design — \"The Corruption\" teaches search/explain/rewind via corrupted L1 island"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: d43002f0-2256-4a4e-8abe-ae97243b5906
---

Rizel approved (2026-06-09) Level 2 "The Corruption" for Planetfall: return to the corrupted L1 island; spreading glitch corruption replaces the countdown as pressure. Arc: investigate 3 glitched artifacts for clue keywords → `entire checkpoint search "..."` → `entire checkpoint explain <id>` to verify (decoy checkpoint from after corruption) → `entire checkpoint rewind` (interactive browser, authentic to real TUI) restores the world. Wrong rewind = soft fail ("you can rewind again") — teaches rewind is safe/repeatable. Include one-line reveal that checkpoint linking is automatic (graduates the L1 y/n training wheel). See [[level1-diner-dash-loop]] and [[level1-done-level2-next]].

lastPrompt: no need to run tests browser test. I'll run them. Also this idea sucks for level 2. let's go back to the drawing board. My issue is that it's too much to understand or consume. The goal is make this s…

content.text:
Here's the back-to-basics pitch.

## Level 2: one new command — `entire checkpoint rewind`

**The whole idea:** Level 2 is the *same game the player already knows* — walk, `git add`, `git commit`, bank checkpoints, beat the clock. One thing is different: midway through the run, a scripted glitch storm hits and **melts everything you've banked**.

That's the moment. In Level 1, "MEMORIES MELTED" was the fail screen — permanent, run over, press R. So when the player watches their banked memories sink into the sand *mid-run*, they think they've lost. But the terminal lights up with one line:

> `they were checkpointed. nothing banked is ever lost.`
> TYPE `entire checkpoint rewind`

Type it, and time visibly runs backwards — the memories rise out of the ground, re-freeze, the ice glow comes back. Then you finish the level normally.

**Why this works:**
- **Zero new systems.** No clues, no search syntax, no IDs to compare, no browser UI. The player does exactly what L1 trained them to do, plus types one new command once.
- **The tension is already built.** Same countdown, same panic sky — and the storm *eats clock time*, so rewind saves your work but you still have to hustle to the finish.
- **It teaches the actual value proposition.** L1 taught "checkpoints bank your work." L2 teaches the punchline: "banked means you can always get it back." The melt-then-rewind beat is L1's worst fear, reversed by one command. That's the superpower moment with nothing else in the way.

**Shape of the run:** land → same briefing style, one new line ("storms have been hitting the island") → collect and bank like L1 → at the moment you bank the 2nd memory, the storm hits and melts everything → terminal teaches `entire checkpoint rewind` → world rewinds → bank the last memory → `entire checkpoint list` finish line, same as L1.

One small design question before I build: when the storm melts the work, should the **clock keep ticking** during the melt-and-rewind beat (max tension, rewind feels urgent), or **pause for the teaching moment** and resume after (calmer, makes sure the lesson lands)? My lean is pause — it's the one moment in the level where we want the player reading, not panicking.

Also: I'll rip out the corruption implementation (`corruptionView.js` + its HTML/CSS) and build this on top of the existing `islandView` code instead, since it reuses nearly everything. Want me to go ahead?

content: We're designing Planetfall Level 2 to teach one new command, `entire checkpoint rewind`, via a mid-run storm that melts banked memories. Awaiting your OK to build it (and whether the clock pauses during the rewind teaching beat). (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: dont make any changes yet. what is the point of the rewind if it's being used in this way?

content.text:
Honest answer: in the way I pitched it, rewind is mis-modeled — and your question exposes exactly the flaw.

**What I pitched:** an external disaster (storm) destroys your banked work, and rewind restores it. That's not rewind — that's *restore from backup*. It quietly teaches a wrong mental model in two directions:

1. **It implies checkpoints can be lost.** In the real tool, the whole point is the opposite: checkpoints are the safe, durable thing. Nothing external threatens them. A game beat where banked checkpoints melt actually undermines the trust the tool is trying to build.
2. **It hides what rewind actually trades.** Real `entire checkpoint rewind` takes your *current working state* back to a past checkpoint. The thing at risk is never the bank — it's the work you've done *since* the last checkpoint. Rewind says: "forward progress has gone bad; give it up and return to the last known-good moment." You sacrifice the bad recent work to recover the good past.

So the real point of rewind, in one line: **checkpoints are save points, and rewind is reloading your save.** It exists because you (or your AI agent) sometimes drive the current state into a ditch — a bad change, a wrong direction — and the cheapest fix isn't repairing forward, it's going back. The value it demonstrates isn't "your bank is safe from storms," it's "you can take risks, because any mistake after a checkpoint is reversible."

**What that implies for the level, if we keep it minimal:** the disaster shouldn't come from outside — it should come from *forward progress going wrong*. Something like: the player banks two memories (safe), then the third one they handle goes bad — committing it corrupts the ship's current state, meter drains, world glitches. Rewind returns the world to the moment of the last checkpoint: the bad commit is gone, the two banked memories are untouched. Then they finish properly. Same single new command, same reuse of L1, but now the fiction matches the tool: the bank was never in danger, only the work since the last save — and rewind is how you get back to it.

No changes made — that's the assessment. Want me to shape that corrected version into the actual Level 2 beats?

message.content: i dont like the idea of using rewind. let's look at some other commands for ideas or even some skills..we have entire search, recap, resume, dispatch..that we can explore..dont implement anything

content.text:
Good — these are actually richer veins than rewind. Quick grounding from the real CLI, then a minimal game shape for each. (Fun note: your original `debris.js` fragments already seeded this — the helmet hints `recap`, the beacon hints `dispatch`, the panel hints `activity`. Past-you was planning a command per artifact.)

## What each command is actually *for*, and its smallest game shape

**`entire recap` — "what was I doing?"**
Real value: you come back after time away and recap rebuilds your context from checkpoint activity — you don't re-read everything, the tool catches you up.
Game shape: Level 2 opens with *you* having the amnesia this time. You land after a blackout — island dark, fog everywhere, beams out, and you genuinely can't see what's left to do. The terminal offers one command: `entire recap`. Typing it replays your Level 1 run — your actual checkpoint IDs scroll past, "here's what you banked, here's what's still missing" — and as it prints, the fog lifts and the remaining memories light up. Then it's the collect-and-bank loop you already know, under the clock.
Load: one command, zero new mechanics. The demonstration is built into the drama: darkness → recap → the world makes sense again. And it mirrors the game's own theme — the cure for lost context.

**`entire dispatch` — "tell the others what happened"**
Real value: composes a summary of recent agent work so you can hand it off — the status update writes itself.
Game shape: the finish line changes. The ship can't call for rescue until it can *say what happened*. After banking everything, instead of `checkpoint list`, you type `entire dispatch` — and the game composes the transmission *from your actual run* (which memories, what order, how much clock was left) and fires it into the sky as the win moment. The value beat is watching a summary you didn't write be accurate about what you did.
Load: one command, replaces the existing finish line. Slightly weaker mid-level tension (it's an ending beat, not a mechanic), but the strongest narrative payoff — dispatch is literally the call home.

**`entire checkpoint search` — "find that moment"**
Real value: your history is queryable; you don't have to remember where anything is.
Game shape: only convincing at scale — searching the 3 things you banked is a shrug. You'd need a fiction like "the previous crew's log has 40 checkpoints" so the list overwhelms and search cuts through. That means adding a corpus *and* a reason to search (a clue or target), which is exactly the variable-creep you flagged on the corruption idea. I'd shelve it for a later level.

**`entire session resume` — "pick up where a session left off"**
Real value: sessions persist; work-in-progress isn't lost between sittings (or between people).
Game shape: a half-recovered memory on the island — someone started it and stopped. `entire session resume` and you inherit their progress mid-stream. Neat, but it requires teaching *sessions* as a concept distinct from checkpoints — a new noun, which is real cognitive load. Also better later.

**Skills (`entire labs review` / `investigate`)** — experimental in the real CLI and multi-concept. Endgame material, not Level 2.

## My take

**Recap is the Level 2 command.** It's the only one where the value demo *is* the gameplay opener rather than a new system: one typed command turns confusion into orientation, then the level is pure L1 muscle memory with the existing tension. And it sets up a clean arc — L1: bank it. L2: come back and recap it. L3: dispatch it home — with search/resume/rewind available as deeper levels if the game grows.

Nothing implemented — including the corruption code from earlier still sitting uncommitted in the working tree, which I'll strip out whenever you've settled on a direction. What's your read on recap?

message.content: okay but what would we do that warrants needing a recap..so far level 1 we just collected 3 items.

content.text:
Right — nothing in Level 1 warrants a recap. Three items, all fully banked, finished seconds ago: any player can hold that in their head. If recap just reprints it, it reads as ceremony, not value.

Recap earns its keep under three conditions, and Level 2 would have to manufacture them:

1. **More state than your head holds.** Not 3 memories — 6 or 7.
2. **Mixed states, not just done/not-done.** The L1 loop already has in-between states: a memory can be *staged*, or *committed but never checkpointed* (they pressed n, or walked away). With 6 memories mid-run, you naturally end up with "2 banked, 1 frozen but unbanked, 3 untouched" — and *that's* what's genuinely hard to track. The real product value of recap is exactly this: not "what's finished" but "what was in flight."
3. **An interruption that hides the visible state.** Here's the honest trick: the reason players don't need recap is that the *island shows them everything* — glowing ice means banked, beams mean untouched. So the interruption has to wipe the display. A blackout hits mid-run: screen cuts, you wake back at the spawn point, and every artifact looks the same — dark, frosted, unreadable. Now the player's actual memory is the only record of which of 6 things was in which state... and it fails, because the blackout was scripted to land at the most chaotic moment.

Then `entire recap` is the cure: it prints the session state — banked ✓, frozen-but-not-checkpointed ⚠ ("finish this one first"), untouched ✗ — and as it prints, the beams relight color-coded. Orientation restored, finish the run.

One mechanic detail that makes the teaching land without forcing it: after the blackout, the player *can* recover without recap — by walking to all six artifacts and checking them one by one. The clock makes that ruinously expensive. Recap is instant. So the command isn't a gate, it's the obviously rational move — which is the actual pitch of the tool: not "you must," but "this saves you the walk."

So the level in one line: **same loop, doubled scale, a blackout that wipes the world's memory at the worst moment, and one new command that gives it back.** The recap has something real to say precisely because the work was bigger, messier, and interrupted.

Still not touching code. Does this version of "warranting a recap" feel right, or does doubling the item count already smell like variable-creep to you?

hookInfos.command: sh -c 'if ! command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then exit 0; fi; exec entire hooks claude-code stop'

message.content: maybe entire search would fit a little better..let's explore that

content.text:
Search is a good fit — arguably better than recap, because its value can be demonstrated *repeatedly* as a mechanic instead of once as a cutscene. The key is what search is actually for: **you remember a word, not a place.** It turns a fuzzy memory of content into an exact location in your history. So the level has to make "finding by walking" hopeless and "finding by word" instant.

## Level 2: "The Archive"

**Fiction:** Level 1's win restored the ship's memory — and the side effect is that the ship's *entire pre-crash archive* surfaces. The island is now dotted with twenty-odd frozen memories: identical ice blocks, no beams, no labels readable from a distance. Everything the previous voyage ever checkpointed. The ship needs three specific ones to plot the way home, and it tells you in plain language:

> "I need the memory of the **ignition sequence**."

**The loop (×3):**
1. The ship asks for a memory, describing it in a sentence — the search word is right there in the request.
2. Walking the field and inspecting blocks one by one is possible — and ruinous under the clock. Twenty identical blocks, one countdown.
3. At the terminal: `entire checkpoint search "ignition"` → the matching checkpoint prints, **and its beam lights up across the island**. That's the money shot, three times: a dark field, one typed word, one light.
4. Sprint to it, press E, memory transmits to the ship, power meter climbs. Next request.

Mistyped or wrong word? Zero results, no penalty except the ticking clock — which is exactly the real-world cost of bad search terms.

**Why this fits your constraints:**
- **One new command**, typed three times — repetition is how the syntax sticks.
- **Reuses L1 entirely:** same island, same terminal, same beams/clock/meter. The only inversion: in L1 the game lit the beams for you; in L2 *nothing lights up until you search*. Search literally turns the lights on.
- **No variable-creep.** Deliberately absent: no clue-hunting (the quest sentence contains the word), no decoy results, no timestamps, no `explain`, no browser UI, no new artifact states. The 20 ice blocks are set dressing, not state to track.
- **The value is felt, not told.** The contrast between "I could check 20 blocks" and "I typed one word" *is* the lesson, and the clock makes you feel it.

And it sharpens the arc: **L1 — bank your memories. L2 — your history is bigger than your head; search it.** Recap/dispatch/resume stay on the shelf for later levels.

One open design choice: when you reach the found block, is retrieval just **E → transmitted** (keeps L2's typing focused purely on the search command — my lean), or a mini version of L1's terminal beat to keep the muscle memory warm? Still not touching code until you call it.

hookInfos.command: sh -c 'if ! command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then exit 0; fi; exec entire hooks claude-code stop'

message.content: are you saying that ship asks for a specific thing..player types a keyword..the item lights up..and the player runs over and grabs it??

lastPrompt: are you saying that ship asks for a specific thing..player types a keyword..the item lights up..and the player runs over and grabs it??

content.text:
Yes — exactly that. Four beats, repeated three times:

1. **Ship asks:** "I need the memory of the *ignition sequence*."
2. **Player types:** `entire checkpoint search "ignition"`
3. **The match lights up** — one beam switches on somewhere out in a field of identical dark ice blocks.
4. **Player runs over and grabs it** (E), it transmits to the ship, meter climbs, next request.

The clock runs the whole time, so the tension is sprint-shaped, like Level 1. And the teaching is in the contrast you feel at step 2-3: twenty unreadable blocks out there, one typed word finds the right one instantly.

That's the entire level. No other new mechanics.

message.content: okay and for level 2..we can have multiple items in our checkpoint list that we didnt grab in level 1 . that's totally okay..just so we can have more options.

content.text:
Exactly — that's the move that makes search worth teaching. The archive is the ship's full history, not just your three: dozens of checkpoints from the previous voyage that the player never personally banked. Fiction-wise it's clean (Level 1 restored ship memory → the whole archive surfaced), and it's honest to the real tool — your checkpoint history quickly grows past what you can remember, *which is the very thing search exists for*. Your three L1 checkpoints can still appear in the list as a nice continuity touch, but the corpus is big enough that browsing is hopeless and searching is obvious.

Let me pin the settled design to memory:

Agreed with Rizel (2026-06-09) after rejecting rewind/recap ideas. Level 2 "The Archive": L1's win surfaced the ship's full pre-crash checkpoint archive — ~20 identical dark ice blocks on the same island (most never grabbed by the player; corpus bigger than memory is the point). Loop ×3: ship asks for a memory in a plain sentence containing the keyword → player types `entire checkpoint search "<word>"` at the terminal → the matching block's beam lights up across the island → sprint over, press E to transmit to ship. One countdown clock for tension; wrong/0-result searches cost only time. Deliberately excluded: clues, decoys, explain, rewind browser, new artifact states. Retrieval is plain E-grab (typing effort lives in the search command). Arc: L1 bank → L2 search; recap/dispatch/resume reserved for later levels. See [[level2-design-direction]] and [[level1-diner-dash-loop]].

Cleanup owed before building: the abandoned corruption implementation in the working tree (src/corruptionView.js + l2 markup in index.html, l2 styles in style.css, corruptionView wiring in main.js). Keep: memoryProps.js extraction, islandView onComplete hook, fp-shared HUD split, corrupted-pin entry concept (pin → Level 2 after L1 completes).

- [Level 1: Diner Dash loop](level1-diner-dash-loop.md) — do/freeze(commit)/checkpoint(collect-the-check) loop; freeze keeps it, checkpoint banks it
